item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this SCP, but something has been nagging me about it. How do the eyes know the window is leaded? Aren't they confined to the site?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It actually got me when the screen turned off to reboot itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And he never even got to finish the entry.

One of the most intimate SCPS I've read. Up there with 1762, if only in terms of building a connection.

+1

item.title: That terminal tho
item.content:encoded:
I know this is probably both late and not going to be seen, but I just wanted to say, mad props for making this able to just be pasted somewhere and it just kinda works. I made an entire Sandbox page just to test that, and when I pasted in the code it just straight up worked. Editing it and everything didn't even break it. Great job on this.

Literally the only thing that broke was the back and next buttons, and I assume that has something to do with me deleting most of the articles so the HTML was manageable to navigate. Mad props.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will henceforth mourn the death of your empathy.

Johnny, get the trumpet, we need someone to play Taps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only thing I liked very much about the article is when the author died. It was just too annoying to listen to him whining the whole time, even though the idea was solid. Gotta give it a -1 for the distraction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*claps slowly and enthusiastically*

In my personal top 50. The amount of production value in this is amazing. Incredible job.

+++1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn this is awesome, I can tell a large amount of time probably went into writing this scp, not the mention the incredible programming that went into making it look amazing. +1

item.title: Possibly my new Favorite
item.content:encoded:
never been more sure on a +1.

Though i got startled twice by the sound of the computer, but even my Anger over accidental jumpscares cant change my mind on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never thought I would see such an interesting SCP page, using Javascript to make it feel like an actual computer terminal is amazing! And it also works on mobile!

I would've liked if there was a constant hum or flickering to make it feel like an actual CRT Monitor, but other than that this is still pretty incredible!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This skip is one I keep coming back to-
Dalitz' tragic story is so intriguing to follow, I ended up getting really attached to his character !
The presentation and interactive aspect of the page is brilliant, too- I can feel the amount of effort put into it.
+1 a million times !

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The production value for this article is absolutely incredible.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's great to hear! I really wanted to tell an immersive story where the format was intrinsically tied to the narrative itself. I'm glad to hear that it worked :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What do you mean?

The "real" Mark never actually spoke to Wade during this. Everything happened inside Wade's head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, how was Mark able to talk to Wade?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't fully understand this until I finished reading it and read your spoilers/what it's about, but man was it a fascinating read regardless.

The progressive corruption and glitching effects really kept me on my toes, I was expecting the screen to corrupt out and me to be face-to-face with the SCP itself at any moment. The screen turning itself off near the end nearly made me jump out of my seat!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very interesting way to tell a story.

Normally I am not a fan of cute formatting, as some authors seem to think that when writing a piece, being clever is the same as actually producing a good narrative - or that cleverness is a substitute for authentic quality.

But this work is both clever and good; and in this particular case the cleverness is part of what makes it intrinsically good.

So +1 from me.

item.title: Wikidot having a fit & Temporary Fix
item.content:encoded:
For the time being, I've set the iframe that loads 4205 to load an HTML file from my external server. Once Wikidot stops shitting the bed, I'll change it back to the local code.

I've reverted back to the prior version now that Wikidot seems to have gotten its act together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was so cool do you know of anyothers like this?

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Second, I suspected that Wade died in the half-second everyone else did toward the end but I wasn't actually clear until I popped in here to read the explanation. I really feel that should have been stated explicitly.

It was stated explicitly on the final document:

Following the death of Junior Researcher Wade Dalitz, a thorough examination of the anomalously archived files has been conducted by MTF Xi-6.

I'm also somewhat confused because all the other incidents seemed to occur at Foundation sites, while Wade was killed at his home.

He wasn't killed at his home. He was killed at The Foundation. He just traveled back to his home in his manufactured mind-space.

Thank you much for the critique and the kind words! I'm happy you enjoyed it despite the misgivings :]

There are a number of things about this that I'm not a huge fan of. First, while the terminal approach is super slick I don't actually feel like it adds all that much to my experience as a reader. Opening clickable spoilers is honestly much more relaxed for me. It almost feels like this would work best if in the format of a game or something where you're reading it in-universe.

Second, I suspected that Wade died in the half-second everyone else did toward the end but I wasn't actually clear until I popped in here to read the explanation. I really feel that should have been stated explicitly. I'm also somewhat confused because all the other incidents seemed to occur at Foundation sites, while Wade was killed at his home.

So those are my two major gripes, but I have to admit I was still engaged really solidly throughout, and Wade's dialog about his old roommate made my chest hurt physically, so I have to give this a +1. The themes in play here were stressful and heartbreaking and I need a nice glass of water and a brief walk afterward :P.

item.title: Re: +1000
item.content:encoded:
Continue being The Best :).

(Well done dude, well-deserved.)

item.title: Re: +1000
item.content:encoded:
o shit o fuck

what do i do now????

aaaaaaaaaaaaaAAAAAAA
aaaaaaaaaaaaaAAAAAAA
We out here my guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, I'll try doing so. Hopefully I will be able to understand it better. Thank you.

Edit: And no, my opinion hasn't seemed to have moved. I just don't like this. The story-telling is well-made, but the story itself doesn't really relate to me, and neither does the main concept and all. So I am afraid its a -1. It just doesn't hit home at all for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reply to both you and Borous.

There's a number of explanations in the thread. I know the thread is quite long, so perhaps it's hard to find.

The key thing to take away is that everything that happens within this SCP, minus the final entry, did not actually happen. It all occurs within his mind. The reason why his "friend" was unaffected was because Dalitz cared for him immensely and wanted them to share an experience so they could relate.

Honestly, I don't want to repeat myself too much from replies I've sent earlier in these comments. I'd really suggest going back and reading them to get some explanations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sitting in the same boat you are here.
I don't quite get what's going on with the friend who's supposedly not affected by the SCP.
The junior researcher's manner of writing annoys me slightly but I know it won't bother everyone.

I find it a little bothersome to wait for the page animations when going back to look for something I missed or didn't make note of while reading at first. Same for the text flipping itself while I try to read through it.

At some point I might re-read this one because I definitely don't understand what's making the terminal glitch, unless it's really as simple as "the entity is about to manifest on it, oooh spooky."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid I am heavily missing something here. I am completely neutral to format screws - I admire the work put into the coding for the JavaScript, but ultimately it doesn't affect my vote.

And here, I just don't know. It feels like SCP-3999 but a tad more understandable. I was just so confused at the end. I must be missing something and it sucks.

Please, someone, help me with this?

item.title: Re: Jump Scare?
item.content:encoded: No, there are no jumpscares :)

item.title: Jump Scare?
item.content:encoded: From what I've been reading in the comments, this seems really interesting but I have terrible anxiety with jump scares. Is there anything like that with this one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved the story and the formatting. Only one thing:

On November 20, 1990, Area 14 had an emergency drill for which the researchers within SCP-048-N's observation room existed.

I'm assuming the researchers exited rather than simply continuing to exist for the drill?

item.title: Re: Was it a cipher? I'm stuck.
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what you are talking about :)

item.title: Re: Was it a cipher? I'm stuck.
item.content:encoded:
Reversing the text line by line and then decoding to utf-7 gives another message without invisible symbols: "lm:XI@6hrWkK>sjnJWx8Q;uG||g;E=]"

As of right now I don't have a clue if either are of another cipher format or if this is all a fluke and I'm doing way too much, but I'm amusing myself.

item.title: Re: Was it a cipher? I'm stuck.
item.content:encoded:
Interestingly if you take 'RWD' as a hint meaning to reverse the text, the base64 decodes to "6&_uFprx>sjnJWxf!g%X}6d" in utf-7.
Every other encoding I found gives you at least some invisible symbol, so I thought this was noteworthy.
I thought the message might need to be decoded multiple times but it seems too short.
Still trying!

item.title: Was it a cipher? I'm stuck.
item.content:encoded:
After seeing the various hex messages you put into this such as 'yum', 'RWD', and 'scp', I got curious as to what the larger block of ciphertext-looking stuff was.
I tried a combination of base64 decoding and base64-applicable vigenere. I doubt I was on the right track but I'm curious as to if it was just empty keyboard spam or actually something and I should keep going. Any hints? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that in the autopsy parts, it mentions the amygdala, occipital lobe and temporal lobes as severely damaged by the eyes, because the amygdala is in charge of emotional memories, the occipital lobe is in charge of vision, and the temporal lobe houses the hippocampi, which hold memories

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dalitz was so relatable I can't begin to explain. I'm never gonna actually write out the SCPs in my sandbox, just left in the form they are. I'll never actually make a full TAS of whatever game I wanted to. I'll never make a song that's any more than lazy acappella of my own voice overlaid a couple times. All my art is pencil sketches, they'll never have color, I don't know how to cleanly scan them online to color digitally, I can never hold a pen to bold the lineart before scanning or I'll ruin my art with mistakes. I've been stuck in college for 7 years with barely avg grades even on subjects I love. It's like realizing what's wrong with me. I just need to be perfect.

Did some math and, considering there was 37 deaths by Christmas 1998, the first death is Jan 5th 1990, and deaths decreased by 84% in the last 15 months (deaths are 0.16x less frequent than before), there were about 70 days between deaths on average, before 4205-delta was implemented. Probably 36 people died before delta, and only 1 died after. In those last 15 months, Dalitz's death saved 5 people. By now, June 2019 he saved around 90~100 people.

Edit: good SCP-048 reference btw

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Funny that's right after a comment from another person (a scientist) expressing just how accurate Dalitz' attitude and dialog is…

Too each their own, I suppose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They're saying that Dalitz is suspension-of-disbelief-breakingly immature/childish/incompetent, and comparing him to a liberal literal child.

leaving typo because that's hilarious

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uuuh, no, Jr. Researcher just means a newly hired researcher. Usually bottom of the totem pole.

Not sure at all what you mean by the rest of that. Dalitz is human just like the rest of us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is really great to hear!

I spent a lot of time chatting with a good friend of mine that works for Oak Ridge National Laboratory and I modeled a lot of how Dalitz talks around how he talks.

Love hearing that it's accurate for others as well :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does… does "Jr Researcher" literally mean "the kid of a researcher?" Like, is every mention of a Jr. Researcher supposed to be some kinda "bring your kids to work day" situation? Because I always assumed a "Jr. Researcher" was some kinda lab assistant or even just an actual researcher who was new to the Foundation and needs to be broken in. Wade reads like if Poochie D. and the first D-class to die in Every Article had a kid, and then that kid got brought to work on "bring your kids to work day."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot, and as an actual scientist who deals with a lot of the same problems this guy does, the way he writes the first few drafts and responds to criticism are frighteningly similar to what I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the most impressive entry in my opinion

item.title: 800+ FELLAS
item.content:encoded:
If anyone had told me this thing would be sitting at 800+ 4 months ago I would've told them they were nuts.

Thanks so much guys, it means so much to me!

See you all at 1000+ ;D

item.title: Re: Similar Anomalies
item.content:encoded:
No other article on the site has a similar format to this one, no.

If you like articles that change upon user inputs, though, have a look at the pages tagged "interactive"!

A lot of the pages tagged "meta" also have some interactive elements, although not all of them qualify for the interactive tag (such as this article).

item.title: Similar Anomalies
item.content:encoded: Are there any other SCPs that you are able to interact with like this one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP article is the "beyond science" version of any article on the site
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Make sure you have Javascript enabled and are using Chrome or Firefox. Microsoft Edge works as well, though the SCP Logo doesn't show up on load, the rest is fine. It does not work at all in Internet Explorer and likely never will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: doesn't work, screen is blank for me after turning power on

item.title: Re: BOI
item.content:encoded: Mostly tears.

item.title: Re: BOI
item.content:encoded: Mountains of code, as well as tears presumably.

item.title: BOI
item.content:encoded: how did you make this please
#82 in the top rated pages of all time!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not too surprised by the Scrollbar issue. On Chrome it uses a custom scrollbar that mimics the look of the terminal. That's not possible on Firefox at the moment.

I'll take a look on Firefox and see if I can alleviate the issue some. Thanks for letting me know!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed the story of article, but I appear to be in the minority who doesn't really enjoy the terminal gimmick. I feel it doesn't really add anything to the story that couldn't be achieved in a more conventional manner. However, I am still very impressed by the amount of coding this must have took.

Also, I don't know if someone else has reported this (my apologies if they have, I didn't see anything skimming the comments), I was having trouble getting the scrollbar to appear. I'm running the latest Firefox on Mac if that helps.

Overall, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nice, this scp is very original, shit, I think the best thing I have watched.

item.title: Very Nice
item.content:encoded:
I’m sure I’m not the first to say this but this is one of the coolest format screws I’ve seen in a long while. Also I’m pleasantly surprised that it works on mobile.

+1 no doubt

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're welcome!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely LOVED THIS. everything from the format screw, to the SCP itself, to the slow unraveling of the researcher's psyche as his insecurities and bottled up feelings are killing him. I really enjoy character-driven SCPs that really delve into a particular researcher's mind and this hit the spot, not to mention the VERY high effort format screw. I'd +10 if I could

PS: I love how you hinted that wade might've had feelings for theo. i'm bisexual myself so it's really nice to see this kind of stuff :>
